text: The surgeon evaluates the rectal prolapse, assessing its length and size, and examines the functional status of the anal sphincter complex. Submucosal infiltration of adrenaline is applied to minimize bleeding during the dissection.
The surgeon performs a careful circular incision of the rectal wall using monopolar diathermy, isolating the rectum while leaving the mesorectum visible to partially fill the pre-sacral space.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection of the rectum, taking care to avoid damaging the visible posterior wall of the vagina, which is exposed with the opening of the Douglas pouch.

Following the isolation and detachment of the rectum, the surgeon applies gentle traction to the proximal colon to evaluate the extent of redundant bowel. A sealing instrument is used for rapid pericolonic dissection.

The surgeon performs a posterior pelvic floor repair using non-absorbable stitches to reinforce the filter complex. The redundant colon is resected, followed by an end-to-end coloanal anastomosis using interrupted non-absorbable stitches.
The surgeon performs a final check to assess the integrity of the coloanal anastomosis, adding additional stitches to fill any visible gaps in the surgical site.
